coredata.dc:title: Rapid “turn-on” detection of atrazine using highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dot
coredata.dc:creator: Mohapatra, Sasmita
Bera, Mihir K.
Das, Rahul K.
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot based highly selective luminescent probe has been designed for the detection of herbicide atrazine. Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot with PLQY 28% has been prepared from hydrothermal treatment of orthophenylene diamine. The turn-on fluorescence of the probe on addition of atrazine is attributed to aggregation induced fluorescence enhancement of amine functionalized carbon dot. The hydrogen bonding interaction of surface amine groups of carbon dot probe and atrazine makes the probe selective and ultrasensitive towards atrazine even in presence of other pesticides and metal ions. The limit of detection for atrazine sensing was found to be 3 pM, which is the lowest among all reported methods for the detection of atrazine. Due to low detection limit the utility of the developed sensor has also been explored in real agricultural samples. Owing to the excellent luminescence property and photostability this fluorescent probe can also be utilised to detect atrazine in bacterial cell by imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon quantum dot
Structural rigidity
Emission enhancement
Herbicide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of atrazine recovery in real samples
Synthesis, characterization and optical properties of oPCD probe
Optimization of pH and response time
Detection of atrazine in bacterial cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of oPCD, (b) high resolution TEM, (c) STEM-EDAX showing atomic percentage of elements, (d) Elemental mapping showing the percentage of C, N, O.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of (A) carbon dots prepared from ortho- phenylene diamine (oPCD) (B) orthophenylene diamine (oPD). (b) FTIR spectrum of (A) oPD, (B) oPCD.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of oPCD, high resolution scan of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s.
(a) Excitation spectrum of oPCD. (b) Excitation dependent emission spectra of oPCD.
(a) PL response of oPCD towards atrazine. (b) Linear range of intensity enhancement and atrazine concentration.
Schematic presentation of plausible aggregation among oPCD in presence of atrazine.
Effect of atrazine on fluorescence life time of oPCD.
Selective fluorescence enhancement of oPCD probe towards atrazine in comparison to other pesticides and metal ions.

Confocal image of bacteria (a)–(d) E. Coli, (e)–(h) S. Aureus, (i)–(l) Lake water, Col 1: Bacterial cell, Col 2: Cell incubated with oPCD (bright field) Col 3:Cell incubated with oPCD (λex 488 nm), Col 4: Green emission from oPCD from bacterial cells preincubated with atrazine.
Comparison of linear range and limit of detection of atrazine by different reported sensing methods.
Analytical performance of fluorescence sensor in real samples.

head.ce:title: Rapid “turn-on” detection of atrazine using highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, Odisha, 769008, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot based highly selective luminescent probe has been designed for the detection of herbicide atrazine. Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot with PLQY 28% has been prepared from hydrothermal treatment of orthophenylene diamine. The turn-on fluorescence of the probe on addition of atrazine is attributed to aggregation induced fluorescence enhancement of amine functionalized carbon dot. The hydrogen bonding interaction of surface amine groups of carbon dot probe and atrazine makes the probe selective and ultrasensitive towards atrazine even in presence of other pesticides and metal ions. The limit of detection for atrazine sensing was found to be 3 pM, which is the lowest among all reported methods for the detection of atrazine. Due to low detection limit the utility of the developed sensor has also been explored in real agricultural samples. Owing to the excellent luminescence property and photostability this fluorescent probe can also be utilised to detect atrazine in bacterial cell by imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: The advancement in the area of pesticides and herbicides for the control of a wide variety of insectivorous and herbaceous pests has been a fundamental contributor to the green evolution [1]. However with the benefit of pesticides there exist some hazardous impacts like the long-term survival of major ecosystems and biodiversity as well as oncological, pulmonary and haematological morbidity in human [2–5]. Atrazine (6-chloro-N-ethyl-N′-isopropyl-1,3,5-triazine-2,4-diamine) is a conventional triazine herbicide with better efficiency to control broad spectrum of weeds in corn, sugarcane, pineapple, and other culture [6–8]. Due to its low reactivity, water solubility and slow degradation, ATZ is reasonably stable, mobile and persistent in the environment for a longer period [9]. In very small quantities, it may act as a carcinogen of type C and an endocrine disruptor of the hormonal system. Continuous exposure to atrazine can cause immune suppression, reproductive abnormalities, carcinogenicity and hormone disruptions [10]. As per to the World Health Organization (WHO) maximum contaminant level (MCL) of atrazine in drinking water and crop is reported to be in a range of 20–250 ng g−1. Thus it is very much important to build up powerful and efficient methods to determine the residues of atrazine herbicide in different agriculture samples. Various analytical methods such as gas chromatography, HPLC, spectrophotometric, colorimetric methods have been reported for sensing trace amount atrazine in environmental samples [11–14]. However these analytical techniques suffer from severe drawbacks like complicated sampling techniques, time consuming sampling, requirement sophisticated instruments, highly trained technicians and inability to perform onsite detection. To overcome these problems electroanalytical techniques have been reported [15–17]. Most of the electroanalytical techniques involve electrochemical sensors based on molecularly imprinted polymers. Although these methods are highly selective for detection of atrazine, it embraces tedious electrode preparation and delicate sampling techniques. Recently, Wang et al. have published colorimetric detection of atrazine using cysteamine functionalized Au nanoparticles [18]. The presence of atrazine can induce aggregation of Au nanoparticles and shifting of SPR absorption band. Even though this method presents a simple naked eye detection of atrazine, the sensor is not cost-effective for large scale application. Therefore, the prime challenges in fabricating ATZ sensor are (1) high sensitivity and selectivity in detection, (2) consist of stable receptors that associate with analytes selectively, (3) retain high aqueous solubility and reproducibility, (4) cost-effectiveness and (6) most importantly low detection limit of the sensor for reliable analytical performance in real samples.

Fluorescence sensing has been emerging as an efficient detection method because of its high sensitivity, spatiotemporal resolution and versatility [19,20]. Advanced techniques based on quantum dot based fluorescence detection may reduce the cost of the sensing process. Recently carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising fluorophores in sensing and imaging applications because of its easy preparation, high PLQY, photostability and tunable luminescence property [21]. The unique photoluminescent property of CD has been explored for sensing a wide range of cations and anions like Fe2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cl−, I-, F- and so on [22–26]. Application of carbon dot towards fluorescence detection of salt ions, organic/biological molecules, and target gases is well reviewed by Sun et al. [27]. The basic sensing mechanism in case of carbon quantum dots is based on PL quenching of C-dot upon aggregation, electron/energy transfer or fluorescence recovery of the already quenched CD-quencher complexes. Although extensive work has been done on rapid detection of metal ions, anions and biomolecules using luminescent carbon quantum dots, the available documentation on development of fluorescence sensors for the detection of small organic molecule pollutants such as pesticides or herbicides is extremely sparse. For instance simple fluorescence detection techniques for few pesticides like sinapine [28], carbaryl [29], paraxon [30], glyphosate [31] have been reported where the limit of detection can go up to ng/mL, sensing methods for common herbicide like atrazine has not been explored. Further, the above reports demonstrate simple protocols to detect different pesticides in real samples like lake water, fruits and vegetables with low LoD, however the detection of pesticides in living biological cells has yet to be explored. The permissible limit defined for most of the modern pesticides are normally obtained from a human health perspective. Even if the concentrations of modern pesticides are far below the detection limit, it is possible that detrimental effects on ecological system exist. Microbial responses have been commended as an early caution indicator of ecosystem stress, because of the quick response to changes in environmental conditions. So we deemed to explore the utility of our sensing protocol in bacterial cells.

In the presented work, we established a novel fluorescent sensor for atrazine detection. We have used highly fluorescent CD as a fluorophore and surface amine groups as receptors to recognize atrazine. The sensing principle involves hydrogen bonding between atrazine and surface NH2 carbon dots of at lower pH. Upon addition of atrazine to the system, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases due to formation of hydrogen bonding between carbon dots and atrazine which induces aggregation and enhances PL intensity (Fig. 1 ). This sensor effectively works in a broad linear concentration range (5pM–7 nM). So it has been successfully applied to detect atrazine in real samples like cucumber, lemon, bottle gourd and sugarcane. The proposed sensing method has also been explored for the detection of atrazine in bacterial cells.

o-Phenylenediamine(OPD), quinalphos, imidacloprid, cypermethrin, chloropyrifos were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Atrazine was obtained from TCI Chemical, Japan. Cucumber, Lemon, watermelon were bought from local market. Dichloromethane was purified by distillation over phosphorous pentoxide. All other chemicals were used as received without further purification. Milipore water (18.2 MΩ) was used throughout the experiment.

0.9 gm o-phenylenediamine was taken in a beaker and dissolved properly using 90 mL ethanol. The transparent solution obtained was transferred into poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. The hydrothermal treatment was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h. The solution was allowed cool down to room temperature naturally Then, the obtained dispersion were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the larger product, and then dialyzed against deionized water by a dialyzator with molecular weight cut off 1000 Da for 24 h. Finally, an orange coloured aqueous solution containing carbon quantum dots (o-PCD) was obtained. The o-PCD was recovered by lyophilizing the solution.

In a typical experiment, 20 μL of o-PCD solution (0.1 mg/mL), 3 mL H2O and 1 mM acetate buffer were mixed and incubated for 5 min. Then 0.2 mL of atrazin solution of different concentration was added and incubated at 37° C for 15 mins. The PL spectrum was recorded (λem = 500 nm at λex = 420 nm). To investigate method selectivity, some common potential interfering pesticides such as quinalphos, cypromethene, imidaclopid and chlorpyrifos and metal ions such as Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+ etc were also tested at similar conditions.

Photoluminescence spectra were collected on a Horiba Quantamaster fluorimeter. Morphological, microstructural analysis and elemental mapping were done using a high resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Technai G2 30ST) operated at 300 kV. The presence of surface functional group was investigated through FTIR recorded by an IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu, spectrophotometer. Surface composition of oPCD was verified by XPS using AlKα excitation source in PHI 5000 Versa Prob II (FEI Inc) instrument. Particle size and mean zeta potential at different pH values were measured using Nano ZS 90, Malvern instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of atrazine recovery in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Edible vegetables such as cucumber, lemon and bottle gourd were collected from local market. 1 g sample was taken and chopped in to small pieces and dissolved in water and ultrasonicated for 2 mins. After keeping for 5 mins different concentrations of atrazine standard solutions were added into the obtained matrix of apple samples. The supernatant was collected for analysis according to the method in Section 2.3. For recovery experiment known quantities of atrazine finely chopped samples then pre-treated and analysed according to the above procedure.

Escherichia coli was grown in LB medium and staphylococcus aureus was grown in NB medium overnight at 37 °C. After overnight growth, a colony from each bacterial stain was collected to measure its optical density and was adjusted to 1.0. The bacterial cells were centrifuged and washed twice with sterilized PBS at 5500 rcf for 10 min, and the cell pellet was resuspended in 20 mL PBS. From the E.coli suspension, three aliquots were prepared containing 5 mL of suspension each. First aliquot was maintained as control, second aliquot was treated with 50 μL of o-PCD (2 mg mL−1) after 3 h of incubation whereas the third aliquot was preliminarily treated with 10 μL of atrazine (7 nM) for 3 h and then 50 μL of o-PCD (2 mg mL−1) was added to it and incubated for 4 h. The three suspensions were kept with respect to standard time so as avoid growth difference in each set. To remove the residual o-PCD and atrazine, the mixture was centrifuged and washed twice to get CD-labelled E.coli. The pellet was then resuspended in PBS buffer. From each set, sample was mounted onto glass slides and subjected to confocal microscope (Leica TCS SP8) for imaging. The fluorescent images were collected upon excitation at 488 nm. The same procedure was followed for imaging and sensing of atrazine by o-PCD in s. aureus. In case of real sample analysis, water was collected from stagnant pool of N.I.T Rourkela (geographical location 22.25 North Latitude and 84.90 East Longitude) and bacterial culture was grown in NB medium. These samples were only treated with o-PCD to conduct sensing efficiency of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization and optical properties of oPCD probe
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots from o-phenylenediammine was obtained following a hydrothermal route reported by Lin et al. [32] and the proposed sensing strategy of atrazine is based on the analyte induced PL enhancement. The C dots were obtained by condensation, polymerization, calcination, and the surface passivation process [33,34]. It has been reported by Chang et al. that polymerization of o-phenylenediammine involves three steps [35]. First, o-phenylenediamine immediately forms dimers and trimers upon addition of oxidants at elevated temperature. These oligomers polymerize to give rigid rod like polymers containing phenazine units which upon carbonization give carbon quantum dot. The polymerization is likely by the formation of radical cations and radical coupling (condensation and intra-molecular cyclization), leading to the formation of C dots containing amino/hydroxyl functional groups on their surfaces (Fig. 1). The TEM image shows uniform CD with diameters of 1–2 nm (Fig. 2 a). The high resolution TEM image shows a lattice spacing of 0.36 nm, which is in agreement with the [002] plane of graphite (Fig. 2b). The STEM-EDAX (Fig. 2c) indicates that CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The EDS mapping (Fig. 2d) shows that there is uniform distribution of C, N and O throughout. The ultraviolet-visible (UV/Vis) absorption spectra of the o-PCDs display analogous characteristics. In comparison to o-phenylenediammine, the higher- and lower-energy absorption bands of the red-shift, that is, from, λ = 209 to 260 nm and λ = 294 to 426 nm for o- PCDs (Fig. 3 a). These red-shifts of the electronic absorption transitions demonstrate that o-PCDs should contain smaller electronic band gaps than phenylenediamines. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were carried out to characterize the chemical bond and functional groups present on the surface of o-PCD. The FTIR spectrum of C dots displays a characteristic broad band between 3050 and 3400 cm−1, indicating the presence of amino and oxygen functional groups on their surfaces (Fig. 3b). Five bands at the wavenumbers of 1270, 1462, 1510, and 1547 cm−1 are attributed to aromatic CC stretching frequencies. A strong band at the wavenumber range 1500–1710 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching frequency of CONH, COOH and CHO groups [36]. The peaks at the wavenumbers of 1070 and 1152 cm−1 are assigned to the CO and CN stretching frequencies, respectively. (Fig. 3b). The survey XPS spectrum of the o-PCD (Fig. 4 a) shows three peaks at 285.1, 399.3, and 533.0 eV, which are ascribed to C1s, N1s,and O1 s signals, respectively. The C dots have the highest PL at the wavelength of 504 nm when excited at 420 nm. The C1 s spectrum displays four distinct peaks at 284.61 eV (CC), 285.4.0 eV (CN bond), 286.8 eV (–CO bond) (Fig. 4b).21 The N1 s spectrum reveals three distinct peaks at 398.4 eV (pyridinic Ns), 399.5 eV (1°/2° amino Ns) and 400.3 eV (pyrrolic Ns) (Fig. 4c). The O1 s spectrum shows two distinct peaks at 531.3 and 533.4 eV, which are ascribed to CO and COH/COC functional groups, respectively (Fig. 4d). The PL property of the o-PCDs was systematically investigated. With excitation wavelength λex 420 nm the emission wavelength is 500 nm. Multiple excitonic bands are observed due to the formation of different types of excitation energy trap on the surface of o-PCD by insertion of N in the hexagonal carbon lattice. Fig. 5 a shows excitation spectra of o-PCDs which shows three major peaks at 470 nm, 550 nm and 590 nm attributed to the π–π* and n- π* transition of aromatic domains and surface states respectively. When o-PCD solution was excited within the range 350–500 nm strong emission resulted within range 400–600 nm (Fig. 5b). The PLQY was found to be 20% which is comparable with PLQY of N-doped C-dot reported by other research group [37]. As per the observation reported by Qain et al. the pyridinic and pyrolic nitrogen atom can break the large conjugated carbon structure and induces the defect on which enhance the fluorescence properties of oPCD [38]. The measurement of intensity with respect to pH indicates that at lower pH the intensity is highest whereas in between pH 5–8 there was no significant change in PL intensity (Fig. S1a). Further, intensity remains almost unchanged up to exposure time of 24 h under UV lamp which indicates that the oPCD are highly photostable (Fig. S1b).

To prove the contribution of surface functionality towards the aggregation of oPCD in presence of atrazine we have done one control experiment. When CD hydrothermally prepared from sucrose and citric acid precursor was used in place of oPCD, there was no significant change in the PL intensity indicating that the distribution property of C-dot is not much affected by atrazine (Fig. S2). The proposed mechanism is also supported by TEM as well as hydrodynamic size measurement (Fig. S3a). It is found that in presence of 20 pM ATZ agglomerated carbon dots of size 40–50 nm been formed. The possibility of agglomeration goes on increasing which has also been verified by measuring the hydrodynamic size of oPCD with change in concentration of atrazine (Fig. S3b). With increasing the concentration of atrazine from 100pM to 7 nM the hydrodynamic size increases from 96.91 nm to 201 nm indicating aggregation of CD in presence of atrazine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of pH and response time
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the intensity of emission band was evaluated in order to explore the working pH range of the developed sensor. Therefore, the interaction between atrazine and oPCD at different pH values (pH 2–8) was studied (Fig. S4). When the pH of the medium was above 4, the oPCD became more sensitive to the atrazine molecule probably because of effective hydrogen bonding among surface −amine groups of oPCD and atrazine. When the pH of medium is below 4, the formation of aggregates was not facilitated which might be due to protonation of surface amine groups of oPCD. pH 5 was found to be the optimum pH for maximum turn on response of oPCD towards atrazine. Keeping pH constant at 5 we investigated the response time of the sensing process. There was three times increase in the fluorescence intensity of the probe in presence of 500 pM atrazine after 35 mins incubation time (Fig. S5). The reaction time has been verified for two different concentration of ATZ and found to be 35 mins.

The selectivity of oPCD probe towards atrazine over other possible pesticides and cations has been investigated taking quinalphos, imidacloprid, cypermethirin, chlorpyrifos, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+ etc as interfering analytes (Fig. 9 ). Vedamalai et al. have explored oPCD probe for the detection of Cu2+ where the PL intensity of oPCD was significantly enhanced in presence of Cu2+ due to formation of Cu(OPD)2 complexes on the surfaces of C dots at pH 7 [35]. However in our case very little PL quenching of oPCD probe was observed in presence of metal ions in acetate buffer at pH 5 in the concentration range 0–10 μM (Fig. S7-S10,SI). We further investigated the PL turn-on response of our oPCD probe towards atrazine in pre-existing Cu2+(1 nM) ion under similar conditions (Acetate buffer, pH 5, 15 min response time). It was observed that the PL turn-on response of oPCD probe is not affected even in presence of Cu2+. In another control experiment, atrazine alone and the mixtures of atrazine and above-mentioned analytes were added into the oPCD aqueous solution respectively then the turn on effect of atrazine, the mixtures of other analytes and atrazine on the photoluminescence of oPCD were compared. In comparison to the efficient turn-on effect of the atrazine the influence from the other coexisting pesticides and metal ions is negligible (Fig. S10).

To validate the feasibility of this sensor in real practice, the sensing experiment was conducted in four different agricultural samples. For this purpose lemon, cucumber, bottle gourd and sugarcane have been spiked with standard atrazine solutions. Non-spiked samples were used as references. For each sample the calibration curve was plotted using standard atrazine solution in same sample extract (Fig. S5, Fig. S6, Fig. S7). The recovery percentage was calculated over a wide range of concentration which has been presented in Table 2 . The recovery percentage varies from 97 to 100% which indicates that this fluorescence detection has high accuracy and can be utilised for detection of atrazine in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of atrazine in bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Biocompatibility of carbon dots is an established issue. In general, the carbon cores of CQDs themselves are not toxic and any cytotoxicity of CQDs is primarily due to surface passivating agents on the CQD [50]. In particular, the carbon dots prepared from o-phenylene diamine have been verified to possess low cytotoxicity and successfully utilised in bioimaging [35]. Hence we tested the intracellular fluorescence imaging capacity of oPCD in two model bacteria cells and also bacteria present in real lake water samples. Then the intracellular atrazine imaging ability of the oPCD was examined. It was observed that as synthesized oPCD shows green emission at λex 488 nm. When the cells were preincubated with atrazine in the growth medium, the fluorescence intensity significantly increased (Fig. 10 ). The green signals from oPCD were intensified in case of all E. coli and S. aureus and lake water samples with increasing concentration of atrazine signifying the ability of oPCD to display fluorescence turn-on behaviour in the living bacterial cells.

The present work has demonstrated the fabrication of a carbon quantum dot based fluorescent probe for detection of herbicide atrazine. Highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon quantum dot has been synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine. This fluorescent probe has been very much effective for selective and ultrasensitive detection of atrazine in aqueous medium. The limit of detection is 3 pM which is the lowest among the reported sensing methods for atrazine. The developed sensor shows good recovery percentage (97–100%) in real agricultural samples even at very low concentration. Our fluorescent probe showed good cell permeability and emission intensity in bacteria cell and thus can be successfully applied for imaging and biosensing of atrazine living bacterial cells. Due to simple operational protocol and high reliability this method may be extended to the determination of atrazine in real agriculture production.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been financially supported by Nanomission, DST, Govt of. India (Ref: SR/NM/NS-27/2012) and SERB, DST, Govt. of India (EMR/2014/000028). MKB thanks NIT Rourkela for providing fellowship for his doctoral research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.155.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sasmita Mohapatra received her Bachelor degree (B. Sc.) with honours in Chemistry from Ravenshaw College, Odisha, India in 1999. She did her Masters in Chemistry from Utkal University, India in 2001. She then obtained her Ph. D. degree from Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur in 2007. Her area of doctoral research was synthesis and application of oxide nanomaterials. Currently she is working as an Associate Professor in National Institute of Technology, Rourkela. Her current research interest includes design of smart nanotheranostics for cancer therapy, fabrication and applications of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, fluorescence nanosensor for environmental applications.

Mihir Kumar Bera obtained his B. Sc degree from Vidyasagar University, Midnapore in 2008, and M.Sc degree in Chemistry from Guru Ghasidas University, Bilaspur in 2010. He is currently continuing his doctoral research in National Institute of Technology, Rourkela. His research interest covers fabrication of fluorescence carbon nanomaterials for sensing agricultural contaminants.

Rahul Kumar Das obtained his Bachelor (B. Sc.) degree from Bangalore University in 2009 and M. Sc. degree from Christ University, Bangalore in 2011. He is currently pursuing his doctoral research in National Institute of Technology Rourkela. His present research interest includes design of novel carbon nanomaterials for theranostic applications.
